summary: Gruber delivers a thorough takedown of Safari 15's redesigned tabs on Mac, arguing they violate fundamental UI design principles. The new tabs look like buttons rather than tabs, abandoning a visual metaphor that worked flawlessly for nearly two decades. The color-matching feature blurs the boundary between browser chrome and web content, creating confusion about which UI elements belong to Safari versus the website. The active tab is rendered with less contrast than inactive tabs, making it harder to identify — a basic usability failure. Gruber argues this represents design driven by cosmetic novelty rather than functionality, directly violating Steve Jobs's principle that design is how something works. He concludes that Safari 15's tabs are a gift to Chrome's browser hegemony.
key_points: Safari 15's tabs look like buttons rather than tabs, breaking the visual metaphor that made tabbed browsing intuitive for two decades
The color-matching feature blurs the line between browser chrome and web content, making it unclear which UI elements belong to Safari versus the website
The active tab has less contrast than inactive tabs, creating ambiguity — especially problematic with exactly two tabs open
The favicon-as-close-button anti-pattern means clicking the icon that represents a page destroys that page, hiding destructive actions behind hover states
Apple threw away a tab design that was best-in-class and inconsistent with the standard tabs used across the rest of macOS
money_quotes: Replacing an interface that doesn't require you to think at all with an interface that requires you to think — even a little — is a design sin of the first order.
The skin-deep 'looks cool, ship it' nature of Safari 15's tab design is like a fictional UI from a movie or TV show, where looking cool is the entirety of the design spec.
A good user interface needs to work first, then worry about looking cool.
The only place in the entire OS where clicking an icon will delete the object you were interested in.
Change for change's sake alone is masturbatory. That with Safari 15 it actually makes usability worse, solely for flamboyant cosmetic reasons, is downright perverse.
Safari 15 for Mac is a tragic own goal — a de facto gift to Chrome and its growing browser hegemony.
Switch to Chrome, the Mac browser where tabs look like tabs.
The first job of any tab design ought to be to make clear which tab is active. I can't believe I had to type that sentence.
key_insight: Safari 15's tab redesign prioritizes cosmetic novelty over fundamental usability, abandoning a proven visual metaphor and violating the principle that design is how something works, not how it looks.
